transcript.transcript: This is the thirteenth day of May, Monday, 1974. My name is David Routh. I'm preparing to interview Mr. Clifford B. Johnson, a one-time Sheriff, Kootenai County, one-time ISP officer, also port of entry, waymaster. There will be some background noise, which comes from a heater that was in Mr. Johnson's house. Also, unfortunately, a few years ago, which Mr. Johnson will mention later, he had a stroke and I am forced to speak up a little louder in order for him to hear me, which will come out on the tape. He speaks in a very soft voice. Also, we are fortunate in having his wife, one-time editor of Coeur d'Alene Press, by the way, assist us with this interview with helping Mr. Johnson remember some of the incidents, dates, and times that occurred during his career in law enforcement here in Kootenai County, the state of Idaho. I guess you don't want me running for governor. What? I'd liked to go to her. No, he was never a Democrat. He was never a Democrat. You're talking about C. Ben Ross, aren't you? Yeah, I'm not. Warchecker's the one that fired. Oh, he was fired, yeah, but he wasn't democratized. That was Siegfried Ross. I was working for him. He was in the... The Thompson policy. In the toolship business for the commercial law department. And we, another guy and I, The right more than county is. I think it's a sub-favorite place. Yeah. Oh yeah, I know that. That's what the law went into effect requiring, right? Yeah. You know, What year was this? What year was that? 1935. 1935. In fact, I've got my first railway license here, and it's the number 22. We should be in the state. Well, how big was Kootenai County at that time? Was it the same size that it is now? Well, no. Kootenai County is? What do you mean, in population or? In actual size. Oh, in square mileage. Yeah. The last division is one that came in 1915, when it was in a lot of trouble. Because I understood it one time, Kootenai County stretched down a lot farther south. I'm not as far as the Canadian border. It was—all your five northern counties then are kind of big. Billington, I think. But Spi is big. But then Bonner and Boundary County separated. And then Benewah was the last one to go. Of course, Shoshone County was never in it. It was never in Kootenai County. Well, How did you get started in law enforcement? Through the driver's license bureau? I had a really good job before that as a manager of the regulation department of the state, which is the state now, which is the territory of the state. Back then it was under the United States. I left the territory of the state and my I left New Jersey to go to the States. And my mother let her do the land. And I was like, oh yeah, you've got to be wrong to keep me out of that job. And I went to him and I said, hey, can I stay with you? And he said, no, you're a great lake keeper. And what they had was a name that they would just say. They used to go out here and name things. They were funny names. Oh, Frank the Rancher? Yeah. He was an intellectual then. Frank LaFrance. And he was dealing with the big boys down there. And they gave me that job to give me my election year. Oh. And then I went to the desk of the board. And I just graduated. The driver's license appeal was coming up. And it was a very, very good interview. 1935. And here's my interview with the desk. There was a lot of applications on it. There were 500 applications in our government over those years. And this is the science. 500 applications, but it was a really neat job. I got the job of an engineer in the United States. I applied for an application for that job. It was a funny job, I think it was. I got one of my kids to make a letter that I was going to be in Raja. That's how I got started. That was part of the instinct when I used to do the same patrols. I was going to go to Sam's, and he stayed for three weeks later. With the Fiat Raja. I told you I started with it. That was part of the six weeks, the six rules, when I was going to close down. I changed it six weeks later. And, the blind badgers were playing, I think, and I was a little bit passionate about it. Even if many of the people who stayed in. Well, did they have radio communications set up in the cars at all back then? They had a radio period. And when I went to work in the sheriff's office, we were just the sheriff and two deputies and two same men. I'm not using hand-holding, I'm not using anything. I'm not using anything. I'm not going to hurt her. Yep. Take care of her. Sure. Tell her what you're doing. But, can you do that? Oh, sure. Oh, shit. And we'll all come and sit in the shelter. And then we'll be able to get in the sheriff's office. That was the entire sheriff's department, then. I think they got more fancy now. Well, Were you working on the paper then at this time while he was in the Sheriff's Office? Yeah, but I wasn't covering the Sheriff's Office, I don't think. What did, what was all the big news back then in the newspaper? Well, I wasn't on the news then. I wasn't, I was working for one of the friends' offices at that time. I had the woman's page and things like that, but I didn't, I really don't remember too much about it. Well, there was a lot of things that happened. Murders. A lot of murders. Was Harry Sheriff then? No, they'd been with the Marshal. Oh, that's right. What? Well, Harry was on the staff then. That's right, I got him on the chief there. Yeah. Harry Heater just died last week. Oh. Think they had More murders back then than what they do now in the county? Boy, I don't know. I think about the same. We don't get very many murders here. A lot of robberies. Yeah. They seem to go by streets. You can get three or four. Period, a big crime center. Yeah, it's hard for me to remember. I've done a ton of that. I don't remember it all. I worked on most of it. Well, how many cars did they allow you? Did every person that worked for the Sheriff's Department have a car, or was it just one car for the Sheriff's Department? Did every Deputy have a car of his own for his use, patrol car, and they have this toy. That's three cars. What was, did they have any special type uniform requirement back then or was it just... No, I mean, we had... Just wore what you had? Yeah. But they would put you in good uniform for the government just before every hundredth. Yeah. But Victor was sheriff. Uh-huh. He had a uniform. He was sheriff. Yes, but I say it was... But he was sheriff that they got you the fight. Yeah, I know about that. Sheriff being the order to get me re-arrived in Texas. I wore a uniform in 1915, 1918. I said it was different. I don't know that word. I don't know what it means. I don't know. Yeah, we used to have a... A... A... A leather wooden piece. So you have a wooden piece. And a green piece. Where did we use... A... A... Leather, what do you use? I got a rubber belt. Leather green. What do you use? Paper. I'm going to have to think about it. You can talk about it. Do they have, like today and everything, all the patrolmen as they go out and everything, they've got the sidearm plus they've got the shotgun. What was your basic weapons that you used back then? Did you just carry a pistol or did they use the shotguns as a rifle? Yeah, we had shotguns. Yeah, but the testers, the test cars, the test units, we didn't carry any of those arms. You didn't carry them in the car like they do now, like the patrol car goes out and has one in the car. Oh yeah, it does. And you had your sidearm on? Oh yeah. What was the main caliber of the sidearm that they used back then? They used the .38 back then. At this pause in the tape, Mr. Johnson left the room briefly to get his .38 to show it to me. So in order to compare the difference of what he used back then as a .38 pistol and what they use now, the only basic difference being length of the barrel and the sight also designed I don't like to use it for using it on the air sack position. Are you sure? Well, I don't know hardly what the thing is. Any questions you want to ask? Well, the best I can do is put the gun to bed. Yeah, he doesn't need to go near me. He's got me over here, so. I don't work much anymore, too. Did you like working for the Sheriff's office? Was that a, once you got into it, Did you enjoy the work you were doing? Oh, I liked it. Until I got this office, I couldn't bring the work business. And relatively quiet. I'm doing it for the hard work now. You didn't have all the problems back then of your search and seizure rights. Oh no. I had to be a little careful about those things. They were a suspect. You just went into the house and just searched the man. Well you had to be a little careful about that of course. If he wanted to beat you down he would have to have been a good color. But he was a good looking man. Well that's good. He was a good looking man. He was a good looking man. He was a good looking man. Now there's a big article in the press tonight about the, it's simply important for Germany to allow the wires to have to be regular, because they're going to have to turn some of those forks and change those reference forks, and things like that. Some believe it's better in 600 cases. They thought they'd have to reverse because of it. And that's the damage that we've already done. I don't know. I wish we had a dam to watch out for. I don't think. Well, when you were working for Sheriff's Office, you worked out of this building right down here on Government Way, right? Yes. You worked out of the building down here on Government Way. Then when you were in, I think it was built in 1908 or? 1925. 1925. 1908 was the City Hall. City Hall. The jail before then was over where the Allies wrote me. And the person that was second is Wallace. Or second Wallace. And then they built this jail in 1925. They had the cooking facilities. Like I know now, downstairs they had the cooking facilities in the kitchen. That was all still that I wasn't building later. Now they hire a cook. They have a cook on-premise hire. Did you hire a cook? Or how did they set up the meals for the people? I think Anna's been retired or she's been here like a year or so. I don't think so. If she retires, might Obama go to the state? He retired the same time I did. Didn't he last time? He had to. Yeah. What year did you retire from the Sheriff's Office? Well, seven years. Well, almost eight. Almost eight? You retired when you were seventy. You retired when you were seventy. Right about now. I had a vacation. I had a vacation. I had a vacation. I had a vacation. See, I got out of the seven-year. See, I got out of the 70 years. I was tied up with a lot of labor concerns over there and I didn't get to retire when I was 65. And they passed a law that they, Retire when I'm 65. And the vast majority of the people, they take retirement. Yeah. The guys that retire in 70 years, they're 50. Well, did they have a retirement, like now they've got a pension plan or a retirement plan, did they have anything like this back when you were working that you paid into for your retirement? No, not until the last six months he worked. Just all he got in on was the last six months of paying into it. Did they make any compensation for injuries that you incurred? Like Now you've got medical plans. Well, you get a medical plan, you're still under the M. The medical plan's the M now. No, no. When you got hurt back then, it just came out of your own pocket. Yeah, the county didn't even pay for it yet. Did you ever incur any injuries working for the Sheriff's Department? I broke my fingers one time. Otherwise, you got away fairly lucky. Yeah, I had one right guy that took my lower leg. And I didn't get to get to her. What was the biggest problem that you had? Like now it seems the biggest problem in the county is robberies, burglaries. What was your biggest problem that you ran into back there? And the homes around the lake that are closed isn't like any other? In fact, I followed one fellow for about a week. We ran him out of a house where it was a dead site to take. And we were just living in the house. This is the tail side of the beach. And we were just, David and myself, were just right behind him. I guess he was in the house. We were just looking for a door. And he jumped out of the window and said, I'm going to get you for your money. And he finally got up and said, Mary, she's in court. And he was breaking the law. She was being picked up. And he was glitching and making some camp over there. And he was playing his flute. And she was over there, too. They wasn't much, but they made me a little camp over there. I used to sleep on it. I used to stay there for a day or two. We used to go out and catch up and sleep. They ruined it. Well, that one house you really ruined, didn't it? It moved all over the place and everything. Well, yeah, that's the first one. Yeah, this is the room where you're talking about. No. It's that room. But you had a bunch of rooms on the campus. And the sheriff's league, where's he at? Well, you found all this stuff up there in the tent. Yeah. Did they have any bootlegging in Idaho going on at that time that caused any problems like they had back in... Back in 1977? Yeah, beer was legal. It was like liquor, but you could get a bottle of liquor and then you could pour it over there and do your shots and stuff. Alcohol division? Yeah, the special office. They had a fishing line. The bars then couldn't sell liquor out of the bars, all they were allowed to sell was beer? Well, they were just beer parlors. Just beer parlors. They weren't bars. They were just taverns, I think. They were just beer parlors. There weren't bars and such. Yeah. There's an exit. When was it repealed? Oh, it was repealed before then, but it wasn't legalized in Idaho, was it? Do you have to put most of this responsibility for rounding them up on your shoulders or did you have any state assistance on this? I'm not sure. I'm not sure. But we worked on the cancer. We had to work on that. But then I think the antigen issue, that was the main issue. The important thing was the reservation and the specials. Well, what was your biggest problem that you had with the Indians back then? Drinking. Just drinking and rowdiness then? Just drinking and rowdiness. I think the fish were eaten up by the fish. The fish had to come down. So mostly, the problems you had, you had basically the same problems as burglaries and everything except for the revenue on the liquor. You had about the same thing as burglaries and everything that you do now, you just have less to do it with. Yeah, I don't believe it's that. There's much else. Gee, we have a lot to be steeped in here. I don't think it's murdering or raping or nothing. It's raping, yeah. Well, I thought it was after World War II when they got closer to the basement up here. Well, yeah, we're going to have to get to it. When I see it when I went to work. Same place as here. In fact, I have a sister who's 18, and I used to ride around with her. I'd be having a harvest. Before I had to go to Florida, where I live, and they have a big distribution of these. But you can get... I had to go to... Well, there's a gala. Seaberries. And... And I'm like, well, I'm very... I had to go to the farmers in Keppel and St. Mary's and Santa Monica, Longsbury, and that's where I was held here. And several days in East Leaf's. I was on the driver's license at Red User in Aron. What was the price of a driver's license back then? What'd you have to pay for one? $0.50 for a driver's license back then. Was there, like now you have to take a written test and then a driving test. What was the kind of test did they give you back? I have this man from Cape Town, I killed him. I'm going to write a book about it. Back there across the street, he had a gas leak on the roof of his house. I was going to have to get a right to a woman's case in Paris, Paris, and they never had any to emphasize before. That is a good, I think, explanation. Probation for the drifter, you're on 33, December 5. What? December 5, 33. I have to take a... I need to take a probation. Probation was repealed thirty-three, December 5. What? December 5, thirty-three. Probation... Was repealed. I guess that's... Well, she was with the minors, wasn't she? No. That's what I said, is... Oh, yeah, I know.

story.description: Recorded on May 15, 1974. Topic is on law enforcement.
